title: Hardy-Littlewood and Chowla Type Conjectures in the Presence of a Siegel Zero
speaker: Terence Tao
abstract: We discuss some consequences of the existence of a Siegel zero for various questions relating to the distribution of the prime numbers, and in particular to conjectures of Hardy-Littlewood and Chowla type.  This is joint work with Joni Teravainen.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Chowla, Hardy-Littlewood, and mixed correlation conjectures
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the Liouville Chowla conjecture, the von Mangoldt Hardy-Littlewood prime-tuples conjecture with its singular series, and their mixed Hardy-Littlewood-Chowla extension, while emphasizing that all higher correlations are essentially open.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional-zero regime and conditional correlation theorems
proof_stages.summary: A Landau-Siegel zero of quality η is introduced, its scarcity and repulsion properties are recalled, and prior and new results are stated: in a polynomial range around the conductor, such a zero gives Chowla/Hardy-Littlewood-type asymptotics, including Tao-Teräväinen mixed correlations with any number of Liouville factors but at most two von Mangoldt factors.

proof_stages.label: Euler-product heuristic: Liouville becomes a character
proof_stages.summary: The Euler product for the exceptional character’s L-function shows that a high-quality zero biases χ(p) toward −1 on primes near the conductor, motivating the approximations λ≈χ and Λ≈χ∗log on rough numbers and reducing Chowla-type sums to character sums and Hardy-Littlewood-type sums to divisor-like correlations.

proof_stages.label: Two-Mangoldt barrier from divisor correlations
proof_stages.summary: The reduction explains the theorem’s serious restriction: pair correlations of divisor-type functions are accessible, but three-point divisor correlations remain out of reach, so the method cannot presently handle more than two von Mangoldt factors.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional-prime estimates and Siegel approximants
proof_stages.summary: The proof quantifies the rarity of exceptional primes χ(p)≠−1 in the relevant range, chooses a threshold beyond which their contribution is negligible, and replaces λ and Λ by hybrid Siegel approximants built from small-prime Liouville behavior, the Dirichlet character, convolution with log, and sieve restrictions.

proof_stages.label: Unconditional effective uniformity via the zero/no-zero dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that similar approximation ideas are not merely illusory: by splitting into the no-Siegel-zero case and the Siegel-zero case, one obtains effective logarithmic-type Gowers-uniformity bounds for the Möbius function and related applications.

proof_stages.label: Extensions, sparsity obstructions, and analogies
proof_stages.summary: The final mathematical discussion considers polynomial Chowla/Bateman-Horn extensions, identifies the obstruction caused by sparse polynomial values potentially concentrating on exceptional primes, and compares the mechanism with function-field and logarithmic-average settings.

examples.name: Twin-prime two-point correlation
examples.note: The correlation Λ(n)Λ(n+2) is used as the standard k=2 Hardy-Littlewood instance and as a motivating application of Heath-Brown-type exceptional-zero results.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the two-point Hardy–Littlewood correlation to Λ(n)Λ(n+2), identifies it with twin primes, and discusses the predicted singular series/error range under a Siegel zero

passes.reason: The shift-2 two-point prime correlation is the minimal prime-gap and first nontrivial Hardy-Littlewood case, used as a motivating test case.
the twin-prime correlation uses the minimal admissible prime gap 2 but is not used to refute a claim

the twin-prime correlation is the minimal admissible prime-pair gap and first nontrivial two-point case, used as a test case rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Goldbach representation counts
examples.note: Goldbach-type sums illustrate how stronger two-Mangoldt estimates with shift dependence can count representations and can even deviate from the naive Hardy-Littlewood prediction in the exceptional-zero world.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites Goldbach counts as a consequence of later pair-correlation results and states qualitative facts about the asymptotic, without deriving or computing a count

passes.reason: Goldbach counts are invoked to show the usual Hardy-Littlewood-type prediction can give the wrong asymptotic in the presence of a Siegel zero.
Goldbach counts divisible by the Siegel-zero conductor are cited as giving the wrong Hardy-Littlewood prediction
Goldbach counts are used to show the Hardy-Littlewood-style prediction can give the wrong number in the presence of a Siegel zero

Goldbach counts are used to note that a Siegel zero can give the wrong Hardy-Littlewood asymptotic.

Goldbach counts are used to show the Hardy–Littlewood Goldbach asymptotic can give the wrong answer when the integer is divisible by the Siegel-zero conductor.

Goldbach counts are used to show the usual Hardy–Littlewood Goldbach asymptotic can fail when the integer is divisible by the Siegel-zero conductor.
Goldbach counts are cited as giving the wrong Hardy-Littlewood asymptotic in the Siegel-zero setting for certain divisible inputs, while the count itself is structurally ordinary.

examples.name: Polynomial values n^2+1
examples.note: The example of λ(n^2+1) exposes the new sparse-set obstruction: the relevant polynomial values could have prime factors concentrated in the small exceptional-prime set, preventing the full expected little-o bound.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the polynomial case n²+1 by explaining how the Siegel-zero approximation would be applied, where exceptional prime factors obstruct it, and what nontrivial bound they can still get

passes.reason: The values of the quadratic n^2+1 form a critically sparse boundary case for extending the method to polynomial arguments.
the quadratic sequence n^2+1 is described as a critical sparsity boundary for these polynomial methods

examples.name: Function-field analogue via character approximation
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker compares the Siegel-zero approximation of Liouville by Dirichlet characters with function-field arguments where analogous character reductions enable correlation estimates.
examples.engagement_reason: in response to a question he traces the function-field analogue through character approximation, reduction to Dirichlet-character correlations, and control by Weil-type estimates

passes.reason: The function-field character-approximation analogue is cited as a parallel positive method rather than a structurally exceptional or adversarial object.
